modules.title: Systems Thinking Foundation
modules.subtitle: Module 1
modules.description: Learn to think holistically about complex problems and understand how different elements interact within a system.

levels.title: Stakeholder Analysis
levels.learningObjective: Identify and categorize stakeholders using power/interest matrix
levels.scenarioText: You are the Lead Systems Engineer for Project Vanguard (Autonomous Logistics Network). The Planetary Council is demanding a progress report. Classify the key stakeholders in the power/interest matrix to determine our response strategy.
hints: Think about who has power to influence the project and who has interest in its outcomes.
Consider both internal and external stakeholders.
High power + high interest = keep satisfied and closely manage.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Vanguard Control, we have a situation. The Planetary Council is demanding an immediate progress report on the autonomous network.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We need to know who we're talking to before we say anything. Engineer, pull up the power/interest matrix.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Classify our key stakeholders. We need to prioritize our response strategy before the public feed gets wind of this.

levels.title: Value Proposition
levels.learningObjective: Match stakeholder needs to system capabilities
levels.scenarioText: Different stakeholders have different needs. Match each stakeholder to the Vanguard network capability that best addresses their primary concern.
hints: Read each stakeholder's primary concern carefully.
Look for direct relationships between needs and capabilities.
Some capabilities may address multiple stakeholders.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The new routing algorithm is facing pushback from multiple sectors.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Everyone has different priorities. Operations wants swarm efficiency, Security wants collision avoidance.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, match these stakeholders to the system capabilities that actually solve their problems. Let's prove the Vanguard system is viable.

levels.title: System Boundaries
levels.learningObjective: Define system boundaries and classify elements
levels.scenarioText: Draw the boundary of the Vanguard Central Control System. Classify elements as inside or outside the system boundary.
hints: The system boundary separates what is controlled by the system from what is not.
Consider data flows across the boundary.
External entities interact with the system but are not part of it.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Scope creep is killing our energy budget. The external syndicates are trying to push their systems into our core scope.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We need to draw a hard line. What is inside the Vanguard Control Core, and what is outside?

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Define the system boundary immediately before we get billed for integrating the global weather grid.

levels.title: Feedback Loops
levels.learningObjective: Identify reinforcing and balancing feedback loops
levels.scenarioText: Connect the causes and effects to form feedback loops in the automated routing ecosystem. Identify whether each loop is reinforcing or balancing.
hints: Reinforcing loops amplify change (positive feedback).
Balancing loops stabilize change (negative feedback).
Follow the arrows to see if the loop strengthens or weakens the initial change.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Throughput is up, but our solar grid is failing under the load. We're caught in a feedback loop!

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Classic feedback loop problem. More drones mean more power draw, which spikes the thermal output.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Map the causal loops in the ecosystem. Identify the reinforcing and balancing loops so we can stabilize the swarm.

modules.title: Requirements Engineering
modules.subtitle: Module 2
modules.description: Master the art of writing, analyzing, and managing requirements throughout the system lifecycle.

levels.title: SMART Requirements
levels.learningObjective: Write requirements that meet SMART criteria
levels.scenarioText: Edit the following Vanguard requirement statements to make them Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
hints: Specific: Who, what, when, where, why?
Measurable: How will you know it's done?
Achievable: Is it realistic with available resources?

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

The drone shall accelerate to 80km/h within 15 seconds under normal load.
The OCC shall enforce role-based access control for all dispatch commands by Q2 2026.
All OCC operators shall complete 8 hours of safety droneing before handover.

levels.title: Traceability Matrix
levels.learningObjective: Build traceability links between requirements
levels.scenarioText: Drag each Vanguard subsystem requirement to its parent stakeholder requirement to build a complete traceability matrix.
hints: Traceability ensures every system requirement traces back to a stakeholder need.
Look for keywords that match between stakeholder and system requirements.
A complete matrix has no orphan requirements.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

stakeholderReqs.text: The system shall provide real-time status updates.
The system shall support 1000 concurrent users.
The system shall comply with GDPR.

systemReqs.text: Status updates shall be pushed every 5 seconds.
The server shall handle 1000 simultaneous connections.
Personal data shall be encrypted at rest and in transit.

levels.title: Verification Methods
levels.learningObjective: Select appropriate verification methods for requirements
levels.scenarioText: Match each requirement to the most appropriate verification method (Inspection, Analysis, Demonstration, or Test).
hints: Inspection: visual or physical examination.
Analysis: modeling, simulation, or algorithmic evaluation.
Demonstration: operation without quantitative measurement.
Test: operation with quantitative measurement.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

requirements: The GUI shall use corporate color scheme.
The algorithm shall sort data in O(n log n).
The login flow shall redirect to dashboard.

levels.title: Change Impact
levels.learningObjective: Analyze ripple effects of requirement changes
levels.scenarioText: A stakeholder has requested a change. Select all requirements and design elements that will be impacted by this change.
hints: Follow the traceability links from the changed requirement.
Consider both upstream (stakeholder) and downstream (design, test) impacts.
Don't forget interface requirements that may be affected.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

config.changeRequest: Add biometric authentication to user login.

items.text: User shall enter username and password.
Password shall be at least 8 characters.
System shall support multiple auth methods.

modules.title: Architecture & Design
modules.subtitle: Module 3
modules.description: Learn to decompose systems, define interfaces, and make trade-off decisions.

levels.title: Component Decomposition
levels.learningObjective: Decompose a system into subsystems and validate N² diagram
levels.scenarioText: Decompose the routingway signaling system into logical subsystems. Validate the completeness using an N² diagram.
hints: Each function should be allocated to exactly one subsystem.
The N² diagram diagonal represents internal interfaces.
Off-diagonal elements represent external interfaces.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Interface Definition
levels.learningObjective: Define data and control flows between components
levels.scenarioText: Draw the data and control flows between the subsystems. Identify any missing interfaces.
hints: Every output from one subsystem should be an input to another.
Control flows manage sequencing and state.
Data flows carry information between subsystems.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Trade-off Analysis
levels.learningObjective: Allocate resources across competing requirements
levels.scenarioText: You have a fixed budget. Allocate resources across competing requirements to maximize overall system value.
hints: Consider the marginal value of each additional resource unit.
The Pareto front shows optimal trade-offs.
Sometimes satisfying all requirements is impossible—prioritize.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Design Patterns
levels.learningObjective: Select appropriate architectural patterns
levels.scenarioText: Given the following consdronets, select the most appropriate architectural pattern for the system.
hints: Client-server works well for centralized control.
Peer-to-peer is robust against single points of failure.
Layered architectures promote separation of concerns.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

config.question: Which pattern best suits a safety-critical distributed control system?

modules.title: Verification & Validation
modules.subtitle: Module 4
modules.description: Ensure the system meets requirements and stakeholder needs through systematic V&V.

levels.title: V&V Planning
levels.learningObjective: Build a V&V matrix mapping lifecycle stages to methods
levels.scenarioText: Build the Verification and Validation matrix by mapping each lifecycle stage to the appropriate verification method.
hints: Early stages favor analysis and inspection.
Later stages favor demonstration and test.
Validation ensures the right system was built; verification ensures it was built right.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

levels.title: Test Case Design
levels.learningObjective: Write test cases from requirements
levels.scenarioText: Write test cases for the following requirements. Ensure each requirement has at least one test case.
hints: A good test case has preconditions, inputs, expected results, and pass/fail criteria.
Consider boundary values and equivalence classes.
Each requirement should be traceable to at least one test case.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

requirements.text: User shall log in with valid credentials.
Account shall lock after 3 failed attempts.
Password reset email shall arrive within 60 seconds.

correctAnswer.REQ-01: TC-01: Input valid credentials → Login succeeds.
correctAnswer.REQ-02: TC-02: Input invalid password 3 times → Account locks.
correctAnswer.REQ-03: TC-03: Request password reset → Email sent within 60s.

levels.title: Review Preparation
levels.learningObjective: Identify review checklist items for artifacts
levels.scenarioText: You are preparing for a design review. Select all checklist items that should be verified for the architecture document.
hints: Check for completeness: are all requirements addressed?
Check for consistency: are there contradictions?
Check for clarity: can stakeholders understand it?

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

items: All requirements traced to design elements

correctAnswer: All requirements traced to design elements

levels.title: RAMS Compliance
levels.learningObjective: Audit requirements against EN 50126 RAMS criteria
levels.scenarioText: Audit the following requirements set against EN 50126 RAMS criteria. Identify gaps in reliability, availability, maintainability, and safety.
hints: EN 50126 requires explicit RAMS targets for safety-critical systems.
Consider quantitative targets (e.g., MTBF, availability percentage).
Safety requirements must include hazard identification and risk acceptance criteria.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

config.question: What is missing from these requirements for EN 50126 compliance?
options: Missing quantitative availability target and hazard log reference

levels.correctAnswer: Missing quantitative availability target and hazard log reference

modules.title: Integration & Management
modules.subtitle: Module 5
modules.description: Master configuration management, risk assessment, and integration planning.

levels.title: Configuration Management
levels.learningObjective: Resolve version conflicts in a CM timeline
levels.scenarioText: Resolve the version conflicts in the configuration management timeline. Place each change in the correct sequence.
hints: Baselines are established at major milestones.
Changes must be approved before implementation.
Trace each change to its originating request.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: Risk Assessment
levels.learningObjective: Place risks on probability/impact matrix and define mitigations
levels.scenarioText: Place each identified risk on the probability/impact matrix and select appropriate mitigation strategies.
hints: High probability + high impact = immediate action required.
Mitigation strategies include avoidance, transfer, mitigation, and acceptance.
Consider residual risk after mitigation.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: Integration Sequencing
levels.learningObjective: Order integration steps to minimize stubs
levels.scenarioText: Order the integration steps to minimize the number of stubs and drivers needed. Detect any potential deadlock loops.
hints: Integrate components with the most interfaces first.
Bottom-up integration minimizes stubs but needs drivers.
Top-down integration minimizes drivers but needs stubs.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: SEMP Construction
levels.learningObjective: Assemble a Systems Engineering Management Plan
levels.scenarioText: Assemble the Systems Engineering Management Plan by selecting the appropriate process building blocks for each section.
hints: SEMP defines how the project will apply SE processes.
Include technical processes, management processes, and support processes.
Tailor the plan to project size and complexity.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: Stakeholder Analysis
levels.learningObjective: Identify and categorize stakeholders using power/interest matrix
levels.scenarioText: You are the Lead Systems Engineer for Project Vanguard (Autonomous Logistics Network). The Planetary Council is demanding a progress report. Classify the key stakeholders in the power/interest matrix to determine our response strategy.
levels.contextText: The Project Vanguard network involves autonomous drones, smart grid charging, public safety algorithms, and airspace management. Key groups include the Planetary Council, citizens, tech syndicates, safety regulators, and emergency protocols.
hints: Think about who has power to influence the project and who has interest in its outcomes.
Consider both internal and external stakeholders.
High power + high interest = keep satisfied and closely manage.

keyPoints: Stakeholders are anyone affected by or who can affect the system
The Power/Interest matrix classifies stakeholders by their influence and concern
High power + high interest stakeholders must be actively managed
Low power + low interest stakeholders need only monitoring
lessonContent.example: In the Project Vanguard routingway, the City Council has high power and high interest — they fund and approve the project. Commuters have low power but high interest — they use the autonomous drones but don't control decisions.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Vanguard Control, we have a situation. The Planetary Council is demanding an immediate progress report on the autonomous network.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We need to know who we're talking to before we say anything. Engineer, pull up the power/interest matrix.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Classify our key stakeholders. We need to prioritize our response strategy before the public feed gets wind of this.

levels.title: Value Proposition
levels.learningObjective: Match stakeholder needs to system capabilities
levels.scenarioText: Different stakeholders have different needs. Match each stakeholder to the Vanguard network capability that best addresses their primary concern.
levels.contextText: The Vanguard system must balance civic safety, swarm efficiency, algorithmic compliance, and energy control.
hints: Read each stakeholder's primary concern carefully.
Look for direct relationships between needs and capabilities.
Some capabilities may address multiple stakeholders.

lessonContent.conceptTitle: Matching Stakeholder Needs to System Capabilities
keyPoints: Every system capability should trace to at least one stakeholder need
Value is created when system features address real concerns
One capability may serve multiple stakeholders
Unmatched needs indicate gaps in the system design
lessonContent.example: Vanguard's 'Collision Avoidance System' directly addresses the 'Passenger Safety' need, while 'Automated Dispatch' addresses 'Operational Efficiency' for the OCC operators.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The new routing algorithm is facing pushback from multiple sectors.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Everyone has different priorities. Operations wants swarm efficiency, Security wants collision avoidance.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, match these stakeholders to the system capabilities that actually solve their problems. Let's prove the Vanguard system is viable.

levels.title: System Boundaries
levels.learningObjective: Define system boundaries and classify elements
levels.scenarioText: Draw the boundary of the Vanguard Central Control System. Classify elements as inside or outside the system boundary.
levels.contextText: The Vanguard Central Control System handles drone routing, scheduling, telemetry, and platform services. It interfaces with external city power grids, national weather databases, and third-party payment gateways.
hints: The system boundary separates what is controlled by the system from what is not.
Consider data flows across the boundary.
External entities interact with the system but are not part of it.

keyPoints: The system boundary separates what the system controls from its environment
Elements inside the boundary are part of the system
External entities interact with the system but are not part of it
Clear boundaries prevent scope creep and clarify interfaces
lessonContent.example: The Vanguard system includes drone routing and scheduling (inside), but the city power grid and weather databases are external entities that interact across the boundary.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Scope creep is killing our energy budget. The external syndicates are trying to push their systems into our core scope.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We need to draw a hard line. What is inside the Vanguard Control Core, and what is outside?

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Define the system boundary immediately before we get billed for integrating the global weather grid.

levels.title: Feedback Loops
levels.learningObjective: Identify reinforcing and balancing feedback loops
levels.scenarioText: Connect the causes and effects to form feedback loops in the automated routing ecosystem. Identify whether each loop is reinforcing or balancing.
levels.contextText: In the automated routing system, ridership demand controls drone frequency, drone frequency impacts power draw, and power draw feeds back into the operating costs.
hints: Reinforcing loops amplify change (positive feedback).
Balancing loops stabilize change (negative feedback).
Follow the arrows to see if the loop strengthens or weakens the initial change.

keyPoints: Reinforcing loops amplify change — they grow exponentially
Balancing loops resist change — they seek equilibrium
Identifying loop type helps predict system behavior
Most real systems contain both types interacting
lessonContent.example: drone frequency scaling to meet demand is a reinforcing loop: more riders → more autonomous drones → better service → more riders. Pricing to offset power costs is a balancing loop: more autonomous drones → higher cost → higher ticket price → fewer riders.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Throughput is up, but our solar grid is failing under the load. We're caught in a feedback loop!

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Classic feedback loop problem. More drones mean more power draw, which spikes the thermal output.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Map the causal loops in the ecosystem. Identify the reinforcing and balancing loops so we can stabilize the swarm.

levels.title: SMART Requirements
levels.learningObjective: Write requirements that meet SMART criteria
levels.scenarioText: Edit the following Vanguard requirement statements to make them Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
levels.contextText: Poor requirements lead to drone delays and budget overruns. Transform these vague statements into SMART requirements for the Vanguard OCC.
hints: Specific: Who, what, when, where, why?
Measurable: How will you know it's done?
Achievable: Is it realistic with available resources?

The drone shall accelerate to 80km/h within 15 seconds under normal load.
The OCC shall enforce role-based access control for all dispatch commands by Q2 2026.
All OCC operators shall complete 8 hours of safety droneing before handover.

keyPoints: Specific: Clearly state what the system shall do
Measurable: Include quantitative acceptance criteria
Achievable: Feasible within consdronets
Relevant: Traces to a stakeholder need
Time-bound: Specifies when or how quickly
lessonContent.example: Vague: 'The system shall be fast.' SMART: 'The system shall respond to user queries within 2 seconds under normal load.'

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

levels.title: Traceability Matrix
levels.learningObjective: Build traceability links between requirements
levels.scenarioText: Drag each Vanguard subsystem requirement to its parent stakeholder requirement to build a complete traceability matrix.
levels.contextText: Traceability ensures every system requirement traces back to a stakeholder need. No orphan requirements allowed.
hints: Traceability ensures every system requirement traces back to a stakeholder need.
Look for keywords that match between stakeholder and system requirements.
A complete matrix has no orphan requirements.

stakeholderReqs.text: The system shall provide real-time status updates.
The system shall support 1000 concurrent users.
The system shall comply with GDPR.

systemReqs.text: Status updates shall be pushed every 5 seconds.
The server shall handle 1000 simultaneous connections.
Personal data shall be encrypted at rest and in transit.

keyPoints: Every system requirement must trace to a stakeholder requirement
Traceability ensures no orphan requirements exist
Forward traceability: stakeholder need → system requirement → design → test
Backward traceability: test → design → system requirement → stakeholder need
lessonContent.example: Stakeholder need 'The system shall provide real-time updates' traces to system requirement 'Status updates shall be pushed every 5 seconds', which traces to a design component and a test case.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

levels.title: Verification Methods
levels.learningObjective: Select appropriate verification methods for requirements
levels.scenarioText: Match each requirement to the most appropriate verification method (Inspection, Analysis, Demonstration, or Test).
levels.contextText: Different requirements need different verification approaches. Choose wisely.
hints: Inspection: visual or physical examination.
Analysis: modeling, simulation, or algorithmic evaluation.
Demonstration: operation without quantitative measurement.
Test: operation with quantitative measurement.

requirements: The GUI shall use corporate color scheme.
The algorithm shall sort data in O(n log n).
The login flow shall redirect to dashboard.

keyPoints: Inspection: Visual or physical examination (e.g., check color scheme)
Analysis: Modeling, simulation, or calculation (e.g., algorithm complexity)
Demonstration: Operation without quantitative measurement (e.g., login flow works)
Test: Operation with quantitative measurement (e.g., handles 1000 req/s)
lessonContent.example: To verify 'The GUI shall use corporate colors', use Inspection (look at it). To verify 'The system shall handle 1000 requests/second', use Test (measure it).

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

levels.title: Change Impact
levels.learningObjective: Analyze ripple effects of requirement changes
levels.scenarioText: A stakeholder has requested a change. Select all requirements and design elements that will be impacted by this change.
levels.contextText: Change is inevitable. Understanding ripple effects prevents surprises later.
hints: Follow the traceability links from the changed requirement.
Consider both upstream (stakeholder) and downstream (design, test) impacts.
Don't forget interface requirements that may be affected.

config.changeRequest: Add biometric authentication to user login.

items.text: User shall enter username and password.
Password shall be at least 8 characters.
System shall support multiple auth methods.

keyPoints: Every change has ripple effects through the system
Follow traceability links to find affected requirements
Consider upstream (stakeholder) and downstream (design, test) impacts
Interface requirements are often overlooked but critically affected
lessonContent.example: Adding biometric authentication affects: the authentication module design, the login test cases, and the 'multiple auth methods' requirement — but not the password length requirement.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: The requirements trace for the targeting subsystem just threw an exception.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: If we don't fix these requirements, the constructor bots are going to build the wrong logic gates.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Engineer, parse the requirements matrix. Ensure everything is SMART, traceable, and verified.

levels.title: Component Decomposition
levels.learningObjective: Decompose a system into subsystems and validate N² diagram
levels.scenarioText: Decompose the routingway signaling system into logical subsystems. Validate the completeness using an N² diagram.
levels.contextText: routingway signaling includes interlocking, drone detection, wayside control, and central supervision.
hints: Each function should be allocated to exactly one subsystem.
The N² diagram diagonal represents internal interfaces.
Off-diagonal elements represent external interfaces.

keyPoints: Decomposition breaks a system into manageable subsystems
Each function should be allocated to exactly one subsystem
N² diagrams show interfaces: diagonal = internal, off-diagonal = external
Complete decomposition covers all functions with no overlaps
lessonContent.example: A routingway signaling system decomposes into: Interlocking (route setting, signal control), drone Detection (axle counting, track circuits), Wayside Control (points, level crossings), and Central Supervision (traffic management, diagnostics).

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Interface Definition
levels.learningObjective: Define data and control flows between components
levels.scenarioText: Draw the data and control flows between the subsystems. Identify any missing interfaces.
levels.contextText: The subsystems must communicate to ensure safe drone movement. Data flows include position reports and control commands.
hints: Every output from one subsystem should be an input to another.
Control flows manage sequencing and state.
Data flows carry information between subsystems.

keyPoints: Every subsystem output should be another subsystem's input
Data flows carry information between components
Control flows manage sequencing and state
Missing interfaces cause integration failures
lessonContent.example: drone Detection sends 'drone Position' (data flow) to Interlocking, which sends 'Point Command' (control flow) to Wayside Control. Central Supervision sends 'Route Request' to Interlocking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Trade-off Analysis
levels.learningObjective: Allocate resources across competing requirements
levels.scenarioText: You have a fixed budget. Allocate resources across competing requirements to maximize overall system value.
levels.contextText: Budget = 100 units. Requirements: Performance (40 max), Security (30 max), Usability (20 max), Maintainability (20 max).
hints: Consider the marginal value of each additional resource unit.
The Pareto front shows optimal trade-offs.
Sometimes satisfying all requirements is impossible—prioritize.

keyPoints: Resources are always limited — you can't maximize everything
The Pareto front shows optimal trade-off points
Marginal value decreases as you invest more in one area
Good trade-offs balance competing stakeholder priorities
lessonContent.example: With a budget of 100 units, allocating 40 to Performance (high value/unit), 30 to Security, 20 to Usability, and 10 to Maintainability maximizes total system value.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: Design Patterns
levels.learningObjective: Select appropriate architectural patterns
levels.scenarioText: Given the following consdronets, select the most appropriate architectural pattern for the system.
levels.contextText: A distributed routingway control system needs high availability, real-time response, and fault tolerance.
hints: Client-server works well for centralized control.
Peer-to-peer is robust against single points of failure.
Layered architectures promote separation of concerns.

config.question: Which pattern best suits a safety-critical distributed control system?

keyPoints: Client-server: Centralized control, clear separation
Peer-to-peer: No single point of failure, decentralized
Layered: Separation of concerns, each layer depends only on the one below
Pattern choice depends on quality attributes (reliability, performance, security)
lessonContent.example: A safety-critical routingway control system needs high availability and fault tolerance — a layered architecture with redundancy provides both separation of concerns and backup paths.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Architecture baseline is unstable. We have unallocated nodes in the N-squared diagram.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: The Critical Design Review is tomorrow! We can't present a flawed holographic model.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Decompose the logical nodes and resolve the interface gaps. I need a stable trade-off analysis stat.

levels.title: V&V Planning
levels.learningObjective: Build a V&V matrix mapping lifecycle stages to methods
levels.scenarioText: Build the Verification and Validation matrix by mapping each lifecycle stage to the appropriate verification method.
levels.contextText: Different lifecycle stages need different V&V approaches. Match stages to methods.
hints: Early stages favor analysis and inspection.
Later stages favor demonstration and test.
Validation ensures the right system was built; verification ensures it was built right.

keyPoints: Verification: Did we build the system right? (meets requirements)
Validation: Did we build the right system? (meets needs)
Early lifecycle stages favor Analysis and Inspection
Later lifecycle stages favor Demonstration and Test
lessonContent.example: During Concept phase, use Analysis (simulate the design). During Integration, use Test (measure performance). During Operation, use Demonstration (show it works in the field).

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

levels.title: Test Case Design
levels.learningObjective: Write test cases from requirements
levels.scenarioText: Write test cases for the following requirements. Ensure each requirement has at least one test case.
levels.contextText: Good test cases are traceable, repeatable, and have clear pass/fail criteria.
hints: A good test case has preconditions, inputs, expected results, and pass/fail criteria.
Consider boundary values and equivalence classes.
Each requirement should be traceable to at least one test case.

correctAnswer.REQ-01: TC-01: Input valid credentials → Login succeeds.
correctAnswer.REQ-02: TC-02: Input invalid password 3 times → Account locks.
correctAnswer.REQ-03: TC-03: Request password reset → Email sent within 60s.

requirements.text: User shall log in with valid credentials.
Account shall lock after 3 failed attempts.
Password reset email shall arrive within 60 seconds.

keyPoints: Each test case needs: preconditions, inputs, expected results, pass/fail criteria
Consider boundary values and equivalence classes
Every requirement should trace to at least one test case
Good test cases are repeatable and independent
lessonContent.example: For 'User shall log in with valid credentials': TC-01: Input valid username + password → Login succeeds. For 'Account locks after 3 failures': TC-02: Input wrong password 3 times → Account locks.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

levels.title: Review Preparation
levels.learningObjective: Identify review checklist items for artifacts
levels.scenarioText: You are preparing for a design review. Select all checklist items that should be verified for the architecture document.
levels.contextText: Design reviews catch defects early. A thorough checklist ensures nothing is missed.
hints: Check for completeness: are all requirements addressed?
Check for consistency: are there contradictions?
Check for clarity: can stakeholders understand it?
correctAnswer: All requirements traced to design elements

items: All requirements traced to design elements

keyPoints: Check completeness: Are all requirements addressed?
Check consistency: Are there contradictions?
Check clarity: Can all stakeholders understand it?
Check traceability: Can every design element trace to a requirement?
lessonContent.example: Before a PDR (Preliminary Design Review), verify: all requirements traced to design elements, interface definitions complete, no unresolved hazards, and stakeholder concerns addressed.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

levels.title: RAMS Compliance
levels.learningObjective: Audit requirements against EN 50126 RAMS criteria
levels.scenarioText: Audit the following requirements set against EN 50126 RAMS criteria. Identify gaps in reliability, availability, maintainability, and safety.
levels.contextText: EN 50126 requires explicit RAMS targets for safety-critical routingway systems.
hints: EN 50126 requires explicit RAMS targets for safety-critical systems.
Consider quantitative targets (e.g., MTBF, availability percentage).
Safety requirements must include hazard identification and risk acceptance criteria.
levels.correctAnswer: Missing quantitative availability target and hazard log reference

config.question: What is missing from these requirements for EN 50126 compliance?
options: Missing quantitative availability target and hazard log reference

keyPoints: RAMS = Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Safety
EN 50126 requires explicit quantitative RAMS targets
Safety requirements must include hazard identification
Risk acceptance criteria must be defined and justified
lessonContent.example: A requirements set missing quantitative availability targets (e.g., '99.99% availability') and a hazard log reference would fail EN 50126 compliance review.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Safety audit incoming. The regulators want to see our V&V matrix for the autonomous braking.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: If we miss a single test case, they pull our operational license. Compliance is non-negotiable.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: Audit the test plans and ensure every requirement is fully verified.

levels.title: Configuration Management
levels.learningObjective: Resolve version conflicts in a CM timeline
levels.scenarioText: Resolve the version conflicts in the configuration management timeline. Place each change in the correct sequence.
levels.contextText: Baselines are established at major milestones. Changes must be approved before implementation.
hints: Baselines are established at major milestones.
Changes must be approved before implementation.
Trace each change to its originating request.

keyPoints: Baselines are established at major milestones
Changes must be formally requested, reviewed, and approved
Every change must be traced to its originating request
Baselines are updated only after verification of implemented changes
lessonContent.example: The CM process: Establish Baseline → Submit Change Request → Review → Approve → Implement → Verify → Update Baseline. Skipping any step risks uncontrolled changes.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: Risk Assessment
levels.learningObjective: Place risks on probability/impact matrix and define mitigations
levels.scenarioText: Place each identified risk on the probability/impact matrix and select appropriate mitigation strategies.
levels.contextText: Risks must be identified, analyzed, and mitigated throughout the project lifecycle.
hints: High probability + high impact = immediate action required.
Mitigation strategies include avoidance, transfer, mitigation, and acceptance.
Consider residual risk after mitigation.

keyPoints: Risks are classified by probability and impact
High probability + high impact = immediate action required
Mitigation strategies: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept
Residual risk remains after mitigation — it must be monitored
lessonContent.example: Signal failure: High probability + High impact → Mitigate with redundant design. Vendor delay: Medium → Transfer with penalty clause. Staff turnover: Low → Accept with knowledge documentation.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: Integration Sequencing
levels.learningObjective: Order integration steps to minimize stubs
levels.scenarioText: Order the integration steps to minimize the number of stubs and drivers needed. Detect any potential deadlock loops.
levels.contextText: Integration order affects testing efficiency. Plan carefully.
hints: Integrate components with the most interfaces first.
Bottom-up integration minimizes stubs but needs drivers.
Top-down integration minimizes drivers but needs stubs.

keyPoints: Integration order affects testing efficiency and stub/driver count
Bottom-up: Minimizes stubs but needs drivers for upper layers
Top-down: Minimizes drivers but needs stubs for lower layers
Integrate components with the most interfaces first to catch issues early
lessonContent.example: For a web application: Core Library → Database Layer → Business Logic → API Layer → Frontend. This bottom-up approach minimizes stubs and allows incremental testing.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-pm
introDialogue.text: We're moving to integration, but the configuration management baseline is corrupted.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: We have version conflicts in the neural net branch and deadlock risks in the integration sequence.

introDialogue.speaker: avatar-lead
introDialogue.text: Execute the SEMP protocols. Resolve the timeline conflicts and order the integration steps to minimize stubs.

levels.title: SEMP Construction
levels.learningObjective: Assemble a Systems Engineering Management Plan
levels.scenarioText: Assemble the Systems Engineering Management Plan by selecting the appropriate process building blocks for each section.
levels.contextText: The SEMP defines how SE processes will be applied on the project.
hints: SEMP defines how the project will apply SE processes.
Include technical processes, management processes, and support processes.
Tailor the plan to project size and complexity.

keyPoints: SEMP defines how SE processes will be applied on the project
Three process categories: Technical, Management, Support
Technical: Requirements Analysis, Architecture Design, Integration, Verification
Tailor the plan to project size and complexity
lessonContent.example: A SEMP for a routingway project includes: Technical Processes (Requirements Analysis, Architecture Design, Integration, Verification), Management Processes (Planning, Risk Management, CM), and Support Processes (QA, Measurement, Review).
